title: I, Mary MacLane: A Diary of Human Days
summaries: "I, Mary MacLane: A Diary of Human Days" by Mary MacLane is a diary-style reflection written in the early 20th century. The narrative serves as an introspective exploration of the author's thoughts, feelings, and identity, focusing on themes of loneliness, self-importance, and the human experience. The central figure, Mary MacLane, is a complex individual grappling with her own ego and existential musings, aiming to capture the essence of her existence.  At the start of the diary, MacLane's self-exploration is immediate and intense, as she confronts her inner self with an array of conflicting emotions. She opens with a somber setting, detailing her surroundings in Butte, Montana, and reflects on her feelings of despair and isolation. Through her candid confessions, she reveals her various selves, blending humor and melancholy. The passage lays the groundwork for her journey of self-discovery, emphasizing her position as both an individual and as a representative of a broader female experience, all while asserting that her focus is solely on her own unique existence.
